subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Assalamu alaykum, and welcome to Unit 3! In this unit you are going to become a materials scientist. A materials scientist is someone who watches how things change. Today, tomorrow, and every day, the world around you is changing. The ice in your cup melts into water. The butter in the pan turns soft and runny when your mother heats it. The sugar you stir into your tea seems to vanish. These are not tricks or magic. They are science, and by the end of this unit you will be able to explain all of them yourself.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 2 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to do all of these things. Read them now, and read them again at the end to see how much you have grown.
Describe what happens to materials like ice, butter, and chocolate when they are heated.
Describe what happens to materials like water and melted butter when they are cooled.
Explain the words melting and freezing using your own examples.
Sort changes into two groups: changes that can be undone (reversible) and changes that cannot be undone (irreversible).
Investigate which materials dissolve in water and which do not.
Predict what will happen when you mix a material with water, and then test your idea.
Stay safe around heat and hot water while you explore.
Connect what you learn to real life in your home and community.

concepts.title: Changing Materials — part 1
concepts.explanation: Assalamu alaykum, and welcome to Unit 3! In this unit you are going to become a materials scientist. A materials scientist is someone who watches how things change. Today, tomorrow, and every day, the world around you is changing. The ice in your cup melts into water. The butter in the pan turns soft and runny when your mother heats it. The sugar you stir into your tea seems to vanish. These are not tricks or magic. They are science, and by the end of this unit you will be able to explain all of them yourself.
concepts.example: You do not need a big laboratory to study science. You only need your eyes, your questions, and the ordinary things in your own home. Think about your kitchen. When someone in your family makes canjeero (the soft,…

concepts.title: Changing Materials — part 2
concepts.explanation: You do not need a big laboratory to study science. You only need your eyes, your questions, and the ordinary things in your own home. Think about your kitchen. When someone in your family makes canjeero (the soft, spongy bread many families eat for breakfast), they mix flour and water into a runny batter, let it rest, and then cook it on a hot pan. The runny batter changes into soft bread. That is a material changing! When your family boils water on a charcoal stove to make it safe to drink, the water gets so hot…
concepts.example: In this unit we will look closely at three big ideas. First, we will see how heating (making something warmer) changes materials. Second, we will see how cooling (making something colder) changes materials. Third, we…

concepts.title: Changing Materials — part 3
concepts.explanation: In this unit we will look closely at three big ideas. First, we will see how heating (making something warmer) changes materials. Second, we will see how cooling (making something colder) changes materials. Third, we will mix materials together and watch what happens - sometimes things dissolve and disappear, and sometimes they do not. Along the way you will learn a very important science word: reversible. A change is reversible if you can undo it and get back what you started with. Some changes cannot be undone…
concepts.example: Why does this matter for you? Because understanding how materials change helps you every single day. It helps you understand why the ice-cream seller in Mogadishu keeps his cart covered on a hot afternoon. It helps you…

concepts.title: Changing Materials — part 4
concepts.explanation: Why does this matter for you? Because understanding how materials change helps you every single day. It helps you understand why the ice-cream seller in Mogadishu keeps his cart covered on a hot afternoon. It helps you understand why your family stores meat in a cold place. It helps you understand cooking, cleaning, and staying healthy. Learning about the world is one way we say thank you to Allah, who made a world so full of wonderful things to notice. So let us open our eyes and begin.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 1
workedExamples.prompt: When a solid gets hot and turns into a liquid, this is called: A) freezing B) melting C) dissolving D) mixing
workedExamples.solution: 1 B - melting A solid turning into a liquid when heated is called melting.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: When a liquid gets cold and turns into a solid, this is called: A) melting B) boiling C) freezing D) dissolving
workedExamples.solution: 2 C - freezing A liquid turning into a solid when cooled is called freezing.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a liquid? A) ice B) a stone C) camel milk D) a wooden spoon
workedExamples.solution: 3 C - camel milk Milk flows and takes the shape of its cup, so it is a liquid.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a solid? A) water B) a block of ice C) steam D) cooking oil
workedExamples.solution: 4 B - a block of ice Ice keeps its own shape and is firm, so it is a solid.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: When you boil water, it turns into a gas called: A) smoke B) steam C) fog D) ice
workedExamples.solution: 5 B - steam Boiling water turns into a gas called steam.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Which material will dissolve in water? A) sand B) sugar C) a small stone D) cooking oil
workedExamples.solution: 6 B - sugar Sugar is soluble - it dissolves and disappears into water.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Which material will not dissolve in water? A) salt B) sugar C) sand D) coffee
workedExamples.solution: 7 C - sand Sand is insoluble - it sinks and does not dissolve.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: A change you can undo, like melting ice, is called: A) reversible B) irreversible C) permanent D) soluble
workedExamples.solution: 8 A - reversible A change you can undo, like melting ice, is reversible.

practice.prompt: When you boil water, it turns into a gas called: A) smoke B) steam C) fog D) ice

practice.prompt: Which material will dissolve in water? A) sand B) sugar C) a small stone D) cooking oil

practice.prompt: Which material will not dissolve in water? A) salt B) sugar C) sand D) coffee
practice.answer: 7 C - sand Sand is insoluble - it sinks and does not dissolve.

practice.prompt: A change you can undo, like melting ice, is called: A) reversible B) irreversible C) permanent D) soluble

practice.prompt: A change you cannot undo, like cooking an egg, is called: A) reversible B) irreversible C) melting D) freezing

activities.title: Observe: Changing Materials — part 6
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of changing materials — part 6 around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: how heat makes materials expand and grow a little bigger? Cooling does the opposite. When materials cool down, they usually get a tiny bit smaller. We call this contracting. Take that balloon that grew bigger in the warm sun and move it into a cool, shady place. As the air inside cools down and pulls together, the balloon shrinks a little and becomes softer. The balloon did not break or change into anything else - the cool air simply made it contract.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: This! Heating: parts move faster and spread out. Solids can melt; liquids can boil. Cooling: parts slow down and pull together. Liquids can freeze. Melting and freezing are opposites. Boiling and cooling steam are opposites too.

terms: Day 1
Read Big Idea 1 (solids, liquids, gases). Find one solid, one liquid, and one gas in your home.
Day 2
Read Big Idea 2 (heating). Watch an adult melt butter or ghee, or watch ice melt on a plate.
Day 3
Read Big Idea 3 (cooling). Freeze water into ice, or notice fat going firm on cold food.
Day 4
Read Big Idea 4 (reversible and irreversible). Sort five changes in your home into the two groups.
Day 5
Read Big Idea 5 (mixing and dissolving). Test if salt, sugar, and sand dissolve in water.
Day 6
Do the Reading Time story and answer the questions. Try the sugar-timing worked example yourself.
Day 7
Do the Self-Assessment Checklist below. Ask your AI Tutor about anything still unclear.
Reversible change
A change you can undo to get back what you started with.
Irreversible change
A change you cannot undo. It stays changed forever.
Dissolve
When a solid mixes so completely into a liquid that it seems to disappear.
Solution
The clear mixture you get after something dissolves.
Soluble
A material that CAN dissolve in a liquid.

vocabulary.meaning: Read Big Idea 1 (solids, liquids, gases). Find one solid, one liquid, and one gas in your home.
Read Big Idea 2 (heating). Watch an adult melt butter or ghee, or watch ice melt on a plate.
Read Big Idea 3 (cooling). Freeze water into ice, or notice fat going firm on cold food.
Read Big Idea 4 (reversible and irreversible). Sort five changes in your home into the two groups.
Read Big Idea 5 (mixing and dissolving). Test if salt, sugar, and sand dissolve in water.
Do the Reading Time story and answer the questions. Try the sugar-timing worked example yourself.
Do the Self-Assessment Checklist below. Ask your AI Tutor about anything still unclear.
A change you can undo to get back what you started with.
vocabulary.example: A reversible change is a change you can undo.

vocabulary.meaning: A change you cannot undo. It stays changed forever.
vocabulary.example: An irreversible change is a change you CANNOT undo.

vocabulary.meaning: When a solid mixes so completely into a liquid that it seems to disappear.
vocabulary.example: Third, we will mix materials together and watch what happens - sometimes things dissolve and disappear, and sometimes they do not.

vocabulary.meaning: The clear mixture you get after something dissolves.
vocabulary.example: The sweet, clear tea you are left with is called a solution.

vocabulary.meaning: A material that CAN dissolve in a liquid.
vocabulary.example: Materials that can dissolve, like sugar and salt, are called soluble.

commonMistakes: Melting and dissolving are the same.
Many changes we see (like melting) can be undone.
Steam looks like a light cloud.
It is easy to guess the wrong way.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What are the three states of matter? Give one example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1. The three states are solid (example: ice), liquid (example: water or camel milk), and gas (example: air or steam). Each state behaves in its own way.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain what melting means and give one example from your home.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2. Melting is when a solid turns into a liquid because it gets hot. Example: butter or ghee melting in a warm pan.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain what freezing means and give one example from your home.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3. Freezing is when a liquid turns into a solid because it gets cold. Example: water turning into ice in a freezer.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a reversible change and an irreversible change?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4. A reversible change can be undone so you get back what you started with (like melting and freezing). An irreversible change cannot be undone and makes a new material (like cooking an egg).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What does it mean when we say sugar is soluble?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5. Soluble means the material CAN dissolve. Sugar is soluble because it disappears into water and makes a solution.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What does it mean when we say sand is insoluble?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6. Insoluble means the material CANNOT dissolve. Sand is insoluble because it stays as sand and sinks to the bottom.

questions.question: When a solid gets hot and turns into a liquid, this is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: melting A solid turning into a liquid when heated is called melting.

questions.question: When a liquid gets cold and turns into a solid, this is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: freezing A liquid turning into a solid when cooled is called freezing.

questions.question: Which of these is a liquid?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: camel milk Milk flows and takes the shape of its cup, so it is a liquid.

questions.question: Which of these is a solid?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a block of ice Ice keeps its own shape and is firm, so it is a solid.

questions.question: When you boil water, it turns into a gas called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: steam Boiling water turns into a gas called steam.

questions.question: Which material will dissolve in water?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: sugar Sugar is soluble - it dissolves and disappears into water.

questions.question: Which material will not dissolve in water?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: sand Sand is insoluble - it sinks and does not dissolve.

questions.question: A change you can undo, like melting ice, is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: reversible A change you can undo, like melting ice, is reversible.

questions.question: A change you cannot undo, like cooking an egg, is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: irreversible Cooking an egg cannot be undone, so it is irreversible.

questions.question: Sugar dissolves fastest in water that is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: warm Warm water dissolves sugar faster than cold water.

questions.question: When air is heated it usually:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: expands and gets bigger Heating makes air and many materials expand.

questions.question: A material that CAN dissolve is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: soluble A material that can dissolve is called soluble.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to do all of these things. Read them now, and read them again at the end to see how much you have grown.
I can describe what happens to materials like ice, butter, and chocolate when they are heated.
I can describe what happens to materials like water and melted butter when they are cooled.
I can explain the words melting and freezing using your own examples.
I can sort changes into two groups: changes that can be undone (reversible) and changes that cannot be undone (irreversible).
I can investigate which materials dissolve in water and which do not.
I can predict what will happen when you mix a material with water, and then test your idea.
I can stay safe around heat and hot water while you explore.
